key_insight: A multiscale PDE model shows that melanoma cells switching between MITF-driven phenotypes can produce three stable population-level growth regimes, including slow growth, invasive expansion, and oscillatory core dynamics.
engineering_relevance: The work illustrates how constitutive, phenotype-structured models can predict emergent behavior in coupled cell populations and warns that single-cell reversibility does not guarantee reversible tissue-scale dynamics.
method: Built a multiscale phenotype-structured PDE framework from subcellular MITF dynamics up to well-mixed and radially resolved population models.
Ran numerical simulations to explore long-term population behavior across model variants.
Classified the outcomes into three stable dynamical regimes with distinct growth and invasion characteristics.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical solution-based analysis that identified three distinct stable long-term behaviors in melanoma populations.
watch_item: Population-scale predictions depend strongly on model assumptions about phenotype switching and spatial coupling, so single-cell reversibility should not be assumed to translate directly to tissue-level reversibility.
paper_title: A quantitative model for the emergent population dynamics of the melanoma MITF rheostat
